chunk-c93021c72fb2fa03f2a5d62bc0adb874.content:
[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=429]
distinguishing features

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=429]
Arms usually break off central disc very easily.   
Distinct spines protrude from aboral margin edge;   
arms long, flexible, flattened and tapering, strap-like.   
Usually five arms.

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=429]
Colour

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=429]
Brown to dark pink.

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=429]
Size

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=429]
Average up to 150 mm diameter, but can get larger individuals.

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=429]
distribution

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=429]
Southern African endemic. West and South Coasts of South Africa, to Port Elizabeth; 54 m to 360+ m depth.

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=429]
Similar species

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=429]
Astropecten polyacanthus and Astropecten exilis, however arms of Luidia africana are more flattened and broader, i.e. less tapered, and break off central disc easily.

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=429]
references

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=429]
Clark AM and Downey ME. 1992. Starfishes of the Atlantic (Volume 3). Chapman and Hall: London. p. 20. (794pp.).

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=429]
Phylum: Echinodermata

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=430]
distinguishing features

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=430]
Inflexible, rigid star with thick, solid, spongy disc. Pentagonal in shape. Marginal plates indistinct. Distinct madreporite. Fine raised bumps (sheaths of adambulacral spines) form distinct rows along each arm, but no spines apparent. Thick fleshy starfish with smooth aboral and oral surface. Double rows of tube feet. No marginal plates visible. Patterning on aboral surface can be very distinct when brooding (see third image).

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=430]
Colour

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=430]
Bright pink to orange on aboral; pale yellow on oral surface.

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=430]
Size

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=430]
Can reach up to 230 mm diameter.

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=430]
distribution

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=430]
South African endemic. West and South Coasts of South Africa

chunk-21ece0344bd87c400d1d234a341d632a.content:
230 mm diameter.

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=430]
distribution

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=430]
South African endemic. West and South Coasts of South Africa; from 400 to 1 000+ m depth.

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=430]
Similar species

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=430]
Spoladaster veneris, but Chondraster ellatosis does not inflate and is more leathery.

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=430]

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=430]
Species identification confirmed by Dr C. Mah, Smithsonian, Washington, June 2015.

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=430]
Phylum: Echinodermata

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=431]
distinguishing features

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=431]
Pentagonal in shape, cushion-like body, often inflated when landed (as in photo), but slowly deflates with time out of water. Numerous papillae coat the aboral surface. Ventral smooth with fine lines.

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=431]
Colour

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=431]
Speckled brilliant orange aboral surface and pale cream smooth oral surface.

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=431]
Size

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=431]
Up to 160 mm diameter.

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=431]
distribution

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=431]
West and South Coasts of South Africa; from 40 to 205+ m depth.

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=431]
Similar species

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=431]
Chondraster elattosis, but S. brachyactis inflates and is not as leathery.

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=432]
distinguishing features

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=432]
Short-armed, stellate body form with a reticular skeleton (spiky skeleton with soft tissue covering). Distinct raised spines covering the aboral surface 1-4 mm in length. Arms fairly rigid, with ends often turning upwards or curling inwards. Two rows of tube feet. Madreporite white in colour, located offcentre halfway to base of arms. Strong spines along the base of arms.

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=432]
Colour

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=432]
Deep orange to red or even pure white, with spines light red to yellowish white. Pale oral surface.

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=432]
Size

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=432]
Average 50 up to 160 mm diameter, mostly small specimens but occasionally large too.

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=432]
distribution

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=432]
South Atlantic including West Coast of South Africa.

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=432]
Similar species

[DOC_ID=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432][SOURCE=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf][PAGE=432]
Lophaster quadrispinus, which has many dense raised tubercles on the aboral surface or Diplopteraster multipes, which is more cushion-like, with arms that are not as clearly defined as P. echinaster.

It consists of two columns: the left column lists taxonomic ranks (Phylum, Class, Order, Family, Genus, Species, Common name) and the right column provides the corresponding classification values (Echinodermata, Asteroidea, Valvatida, Poraniidae, Chondraster, elattosis, Pentagon star). The data points are specific: Phylum Echinodermata, Class Asteroidea, Order Valvatida, Family Poraniidae, Genus Chondraster, Species elattosis, Common name Pentagon star. Footnotes: doc_id=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432; page=431; source=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf Analysis: The table is a taxonomic classification table for the starfish species Spoladaster veneris, with the code name 'SpoBra'. It is organized in a two-column format: the left column lists taxonomic ranks (Phylum, Class, Order, Family, Genus, Species) and a final row for the common name; the right column provides the corresponding scientific or common name. The header row merges both columns to display the species name 'Spoladaster veneris (SpoBra)'. Key data points include: Phylum Echinodermata, Class Asteroidea, Order Valvatida, Family Poraniidae, Genus Spoladaster, Species veneris, and Common name 'Inflated star'. No numeric data or statistical trends are present. The table's significance lies in its role as a formal taxonomic identification for the species described in the surrounding text. The surrounding context compares Spoladaster veneris with two other starfish: Chondraster ellatosis (which 'does not inflate and is more leathery') and Spoladaster brachyactis (which 'inflates and is not as leathery'). The common name 'Inflated star' directly aligns with the inflation trait mentioned for Spoladaster brachyactis and the similar species notes. The table thus supports the surrounding descriptive content by providing the systematic classification, confirming that Spoladaster veneris belongs to the same genus as S. brachyactis (Spoladaster) and to a different family than Chondraster, illustrating the taxonomic relationships underlying the morphological comparisons.

Footnotes: doc_id=doc_sa_invertebrates_p0429_0432; page=432; source=Field-Guide-to-SA-Offshore-Marine-Invertebrates_web-full-version_compressed.pdf Analysis: The table is a taxonomic classification for the species Poraniopsis echinaster, commonly known as the Spiky cushion star. It is structured as a two-column key-value list, with the left column containing hierarchical taxonomic ranks and the right column providing the specific classification for this organism. The ranks progress from broadest (Phylum: Echinodermata) to most specific (Species: echinaster), also including Class: Asteriodea (a common misspelling of Asteroidea), Order: Valvatida, Family: Poraniidae, Genus: Poraniopsis, and the Common name: Spiky cushion star. This taxonomy places the species within the starfish group (asteroids) and the family Poraniidae, which includes cushion-like starfish. The surrounding content on page 432 describes the species' distinguishing features: short-armed, stellate body with a reticular skeleton and distinct raised spines 1–4 mm in length; colour ranging from deep orange to red or pure white with light red to yellowish white spines; size averaging 50 mm up to 160 mm diameter; distribution limited to the South Atlantic including the West Coast of South Africa; and similar species such as Lophaster quadrispinus and Diplopteraster multipes. The table supports this description by providing the scientific identity for the organism, confirming that the morphological and ecological data refer to Poraniopsis echinaster. The common name 'Spiky cushion star' directly aligns with the presence of raised spines and a cushion-like body form. The table also links to referenced works (Clark & Courtman-Stock 1976, Clark & Downey 1992) and a species identification confirmation by Dr. C. Mah in 2015, establishing the taxonomic authority. Key data points include the specific ranks and the common name, which together enable precise identification within the field guide's context.
